planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, average to poor soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; bigroot geranium tolerates dry shade better than almost any other groundcover and spreads steadily by surface-running rhizomes.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor soil in partial to full shade; bigroot geranium is arguably the best dry-shade groundcover for temperate gardens, producing a dense, weed-suppressing mat of aromatic, lobed leaves that color beautifully in fall, and producing magenta-pink to white flowers in late spring to early summer. It is one of very few ornamentals that thrives in the challenging dry shade under mature maples and beeches where little else will grow. The sticky, scented foliage is deer-resistant. Semi-evergreen in mild winters. Surface rhizomes spread steadily without becoming invasive. Divide in spring or fall to expand plantings. The aromatic leaves were historically used as a geranium oil source in the perfume industry.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; mourning widow is one of the most shade-tolerant of all geraniums and self-sows freely to naturalize in shaded situations.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; mourning widow produces small, dark, reflexed-petaled flowers in deep purple-maroon (rarely white or lilac) in late spring to early summer — the somber dark color and backward-bending petals account for the mournful common name. It is the most shade-tolerant geranium species, performing well even in the dense dry shade of evergreens. After bloom, cut the entire plant back by half to prompt fresh foliage growth; the leaves re-emerge quickly and remain attractive all season. Self-sows generously to maintain a naturalized presence in shaded woodland gardens. 'Samobor' has strikingly chocolate-marked leaves that are ornamental throughout the growing season.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, poor to average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; blood-red cranesbill is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established and performs well in alkaline, rocky soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained, poor to average soil in full sun to partial shade; blood-red cranesbill forms a low, compact mound of deeply dissected, dark-green leaves covered in bright magenta-crimson flowers from late spring through summer — with repeat bloom after shearing — and then turns brilliant orange-red in fall, making it a three-season performer. It is unusually drought-tolerant and lime-tolerant for a geranium, thriving in rocky banks, wall crevices, and gravelly soils where most plants struggle. Shear plants by half in midsummer after the main bloom flush to prompt fresh growth and a second round of flowers in late summer. 'Album' (white) and 'Striatum' (pale pink with pink veins) are classic selections. Long-lived and requires essentially no maintenance.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; meadow cranesbill is taller and more vigorous than most hardy geraniums and benefits from staking in exposed sites.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; meadow cranesbill is the most robust of the hardy geraniums, forming large clumps that produce clear blue to violet-blue 1-inch flowers with dark veining from early to midsummer in outstanding abundance. After flowering, cut the entire plant back to the ground — it regrows within 3–4 weeks and produces fresh, attractive foliage for the rest of the season, sometimes with a light second flush of bloom. 'Mrs. Kendall Clark' (pale blue), 'Plenum Violaceum' (double purple), and 'Johnson's Blue' (a hybrid) are classic cultivars. Self-sows in suitable conditions; deadhead promptly to prevent seeding if unwanted. Excellent for naturalistic meadow and cottage garden plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; Japanese sedge provides year-round evergreen foliage and tolerates a wide range of soil conditions including clay and dry shade.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese sedge forms graceful, arching clumps of narrow, dark-green, evergreen leaves that provide year-round texture in shaded borders and woodland gardens where most ornamental grasses cannot thrive. The gold-variegated selections 'Gilt' and 'Fisher's Form' provide bright foliage contrast in deep shade that few other plants match. Plants are low-maintenance once established, requiring only the removal of tattered older leaves in early spring. Tolerates a wide range of soil conditions including clay, alkaline soil, and periodic dry spells once established. Divide in spring every 4–5 years if clumps become congested. Excellent as a shade groundcover in mass plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; evergold sedge provides persistent chartreuse-yellow-and-green foliage that brightens dark shaded corners year-round.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; evergold sedge is one of the most popular shade foliage plants in cultivation, producing dense, arching clumps of narrow leaves with a bright yellow-gold central stripe and dark green edges that glow with captured light even in deep shade. The evergreen foliage provides color through winter and is at its most vibrant in spring, making it an invaluable element in shade gardens for pairing with purple-leaved heucheras, blue hostas, and dark-leaved shrubs. Virtually maintenance-free beyond removing the oldest leaves in early spring. Tolerates clay soil and periodic drought once established. Excellent in containers and as a graceful edging plant along shaded paths.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; golden alexanders naturalizes by self-seeding in wet meadows, woodland edges, and rain garden plantings.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun near ponds, streams, or in rain gardens; golden alexanders is a native Eastern North American prairie and woodland-edge plant that produces flat-topped, compound flower clusters of bright yellow flowers in mid-spring — among the earliest wildflowers to bloom after trout lily and bloodroot — and is a larval host plant for the black swallowtail butterfly, whose caterpillars feed on the carrot-family foliage. The plant tolerates both moist and moderately dry conditions and performs well in the challenging conditions of rain gardens. Attractive glossy seed heads persist into summer. Self-sows freely to naturalize and fill shaded, moist areas over time.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in early fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade to partial shade; false hellebore is extremely slow from division or seed but is essentially permanent once established in suitable cool, wet woodland conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in early fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade to partial shade beside streams, bogs, or in wet woodland gardens; false hellebore is an extraordinarily bold native perennial of Eastern and Pacific North American cool, wet forests, producing massive, prominently ribbed and pleated leaves up to 12 inches wide that unfurl from fat buds in early spring in a display reminiscent of a Jurassic fern. Tall spikes of small green flowers appear in midsummer on plants in established colonies. The plant is extremely toxic (all parts) — do not handle without gloves and never allow ingestion — but its dramatic foliage adds unparalleled texture to cool, shaded, wet sites where it requires zero maintenance once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
